---
title: "Week 10 – ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)"
subtitle: "STAT9004: Regression Analysis"
---

> 📝 **Study Guide Notes** — These notes are designed to be beginner-friendly.
> Leave space below each section to add your own annotations and examples as you review.

---

# Key Topics Covered This Week

- What ANCOVA is and why we use it
- The difference between a **covariate** and a **factor**
- The ANCOVA model formula (with and without interaction)
- How to identify the **experimental design** in a question
- Model **assumptions** (independence, normality, equal variance)
- How to **interpret R output** from an ANCOVA model
- Hypothesis testing for each term in the model
- The **Parsimony Principle** — dropping non-significant terms
- Comparing **R²** vs **Adjusted R²**

---

# What is ANCOVA?

## The Big Picture

**ANCOVA** stands for **Analysis of Covariance**. The Professor put it very simply in class:

> *"It's ANOVA with a C — and the C stands for Covariance."*

Think of it this way:

- **ANOVA** = you have a **categorical** explanatory variable (like treatment groups)
- **Linear Regression** = you have a **continuous** (quantitative) explanatory variable
- **ANCOVA** = you have **both at the same time**

ANCOVA is the right tool when:

- You have **one main categorical variable** you care about most (e.g., grazed vs ungrazed)
- You also have a **continuous variable** that influences the outcome but isn't your main focus — this is called the **covariate**

## What is a Covariate?

A **covariate** is a continuous (quantitative) variable that:

- You're **not primarily interested in**, but
- You **include in the model** to **control for its effect** on the response variable

> **Why include it if we don't care about it?**  
> Because ignoring it would give you misleading results. The covariate "adjusts" the comparison between your groups, making it fairer.

---

# The Fruit & Grazing Example

## The Experiment

This is the main example used throughout the lecture. Make sure you understand it well — the Professor said exam questions will give you R output like this and ask you to interpret it.

**Setup:**

- 40 plants were split into two groups:
  - **Grazed** — exposed to rabbits for the first two weeks of growth
  - **Ungrazed** — left alone
- At the end of the season, **fruit production** (dry weight in mg) was recorded

**The Variables:**

| Variable | Type | Role |
|----------|------|------|
| `Fruit` | Continuous | **Response variable** (what we're measuring) |
| `Grazing` | Categorical (2 levels: Grazed / Ungrazed) | **Main explanatory variable** |
| `Root` | Continuous | **Covariate** (initial plant size — diameter of rootstock) |

## Why Not Just Do a t-test?

Your first instinct might be: *"Just compare grazed vs ungrazed fruit production with a t-test."*

**This is wrong** — and the Professor emphasized this.

The problem is: **the initial plant size (Root) also affects fruit production**. Ignoring this means your comparison between grazed and ungrazed is not fair — larger plants will produce more fruit regardless of grazing.

> ⚠️ **Exam tip:** If asked "why not use a t-test here?" — your answer is that the amount of fruit produced may also depend on the initial plant size, so we must control for it.

## The Counter-Intuitive Result

Without ANCOVA, a simple t-test suggests that **grazed plants produce MORE fruit** than ungrazed. This seems wrong! Rabbits eating the plants shouldn't increase fruit production.

This misleading result happens because the **grazed plants happened to be larger** to begin with. ANCOVA corrects for this by accounting for initial size.

---

# The Experimental Design

The Professor asked students to identify the experimental design — this is an **exam-style question**.

## Key Design Terminology

**Experimental units** = the individual plants (40 of them)

**Factor** = Grazing (the independent variable)

**Levels of the factor** = Grazed and Ungrazed (2 levels = 2 treatments)

**Covariate** = Initial plant size (Root) — this is continuous

**Response variable** = Fruit production (continuous)

## Design Classification

This experiment is:

- A **Single-Factor Design** — only one factor (Grazing)
- A **Completely Randomized Design (CRD)** — each plant has an equal chance of being assigned to either treatment

> **What is Completely Randomized?** It means each experimental unit (plant) has the same chance of receiving any treatment. The treatment is assigned at random — no pattern or structure.

## What Would Make This a Factorial Design?

The Professor discussed this as a comparison point:

| Design | What it requires |
|--------|-----------------|
| Single-factor | 1 factor with 2+ levels |
| Factorial | 2+ factors, each with 2+ levels |
| Full Factorial | Tests **all possible combinations** of factor levels (all main effects + all interaction effects) |

> ⚠️ **Exam tip:** Be able to identify what type of design is being used, and what would need to change to make it a different type.

---

# Assumptions

These assumptions apply to ANCOVA (same as other linear models). The Professor said:

> *"Ensuring these assumptions are valid is an extremely important part of the design of the experiment."*

## 1. Independence

- Errors (residuals) must be independent of each other
- **How to check:** Plot residuals in time order — positive correlation = violation
- **How to ensure:** Proper **randomisation** at the start of the experiment

## 2. Normality

- The errors should follow a normal distribution
- **How to check:**
  - **QQ plot** (quantile-quantile plot) — should show a straight line
  - **Histogram** of residuals — should show a bell-shaped curve
  - **Statistical tests:** Kolmogorov-Smirnov or Shapiro-Wilk tests

> 💡 The histogram is better for large datasets. The QQ plot is commonly used and you should be able to interpret it.

## 3. Homogeneity of Variance (Equal Variance)

- The variance of residuals should be equal across groups
- **How to check:**
  - **Levene's test** (statistical test)
  - **Residuals vs Fitted values plot** — should show no pattern, centred around zero

> ⚠️ **Exam tip:** The Professor said *"make sure you're able to interpret diagnostic plots from R."* Specifically: QQ plots, residuals vs fitted, and histograms.

---

# The ANCOVA Model

## Full Model (with Interaction)

$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 Z + \beta_3 XZ + e$$

Where:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $Y$ | Response variable (Fruit production) |
| $X$ | **Continuous** explanatory variable (Root size) |
| $Z$ | **Dummy variable** for the categorical factor (0 = Grazed, 1 = Ungrazed) |
| $XZ$ | **Interaction term** between Root and Grazing |
| $e$ | Error term |

## Interpreting Each Coefficient

| Coefficient | Meaning |
|-------------|---------|
| $\beta_0$ | Intercept for the **reference group** (Grazed plants, since Z=0) |
| $\beta_1$ | Effect of a 1-unit increase in Root on Fruit, **for Grazed plants** |
| $\beta_2$ | Change in intercept when switching from **Grazed → Ungrazed** |
| $\beta_3$ | Change in **slope** when switching from Grazed → Ungrazed (the interaction effect) |

## What the Model Looks Like for Each Group

**For Grazed plants** (Z = 0):

$$\text{Fruit} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{Root} + e$$

**For Ungrazed plants** (Z = 1):

$$\text{Fruit} = (\beta_0 + \beta_2) + (\beta_1 + \beta_3) \cdot \text{Root} + e$$

> 💡 **Plain English:** Grazed is the reference group. For ungrazed plants, both the intercept and the slope are adjusted by adding $\beta_2$ and $\beta_3$ respectively.

---

# Reading the R Output

## Full Model (with Interaction)

Here is the R output from running `lm(Fruit ~ Root * Grazing, data = fruit)`:

```
Coefficients:
                    Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)         -125.173    12.811   -9.771  1.15e-11 ***
Root                  23.240     1.531   15.182  < 2e-16 ***
GrazingUngrazed       30.806    16.842    1.829   0.0757 .
Root:GrazingUngrazed   0.756     2.354    0.321   0.7500

Residual standard error: 6.831 on 36 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9293,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9234
F-statistic: 157.6 on 3 and 36 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
```

### Step-by-Step Interpretation

> ⚠️ **The Professor's key advice on how to read output — this is exam-critical:**

**Step 1 — Overall model (F-statistic at the bottom):**
The p-value is < 2.2e-16, which is much less than 0.05. The **overall model is statistically significant**. This means at least one predictor has an effect.

**Step 2 — Interaction term first:**
Look at `Root:GrazingUngrazed`. The p-value is 0.75 — **not significant** (> 0.05). This means the slopes for grazed and ungrazed plants are **not different** from each other (the lines are parallel).

> 💡 The interaction term tells us: *does the effect of Root on Fruit CHANGE depending on whether the plant is grazed or not?* If not significant → it doesn't change → the lines are parallel.

**Step 3 — Main effects:**
- `Root`: p < 2e-16 → **highly significant** ✅
- `GrazingUngrazed`: p = 0.0757 → **not significant** at the 5% level ❌ (close, but over 0.05)

## The Fitted Equations

**Overall model:**
$$\hat{\text{Fruit}} = -125.173 + 23.24 \cdot \text{Root} + 30.806 \cdot Z + 0.756 \cdot Z \cdot \text{Root}$$

**Grazed plants** (Z = 0):
$$\hat{\text{Fruit}} = -125.173 + 23.24 \cdot \text{Root}$$

**Ungrazed plants** (Z = 1):
$$\hat{\text{Fruit}} = -94.367 + 23.996 \cdot \text{Root}$$

*(The intercept becomes −125.173 + 30.806 = −94.367, and the slope becomes 23.24 + 0.756 = 23.996)*

---

# Hypothesis Tests

The Professor emphasised this: *"In a lot of exam questions, they will ask you to state the model and state the hypotheses."*

## Hypotheses You Need to Know

### 1. Overall Model
$$H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \beta_3 = 0 \quad \text{(no predictor has an effect)}$$
$$H_A: \text{at least one coefficient} \neq 0$$

### 2. Interaction Term (Test for parallel slopes)
$$H_0: \beta_3 = 0 \quad \text{(slopes are the same — lines are parallel)}$$
$$H_A: \beta_3 \neq 0 \quad \text{(slopes differ)}$$

### 3. Root (Covariate)
$$H_0: \beta_{\text{Root}} = 0 \quad \text{(Root has no effect)}$$
$$H_A: \beta_{\text{Root}} \neq 0$$

### 4. Grazing (Factor)
$$H_0: \beta_{\text{Grazing}} = 0 \quad \text{(Grazing has no effect on the intercept)}$$
$$H_A: \beta_{\text{Grazing}} \neq 0$$

### 5. Do the two regression lines coincide? (same slope AND intercept)
$$H_0: \beta_2 = \beta_3 = 0$$
$$H_A: \beta_2 \neq 0 \text{ or } \beta_3 \neq 0$$

> 💡 **Plain English on the interaction test:** Testing $H_0: \beta_3 = 0$ is the same as asking *"Are the two regression lines parallel?"* If we fail to reject $H_0$, it means we cannot say the slopes are different — they are effectively parallel.

---

# The Parsimony Principle

## What It Means

> *"If we have two or more possible models, each of which fit the data adequately, the one with the fewest explanatory variables is preferred."* — Prof. Harrison

In simple terms: **simpler is better**, as long as the simpler model fits the data just as well.

## When to Drop a Term

- When a term is **not statistically significant**, it may not be contributing to the model
- Drop terms starting with the one with the **highest p-value** (most insignificant)
- After dropping, rerun the model and reassess

> ⚠️ **Important nuance the Professor highlighted:** This isn't a hard rule. If you have a scientific reason to keep a term, you can keep it even if it's not significant. It depends on the context.

## Comparing R² and Adjusted R²

After removing the interaction term, compare the two models:

| Metric | With Interaction | Without Interaction |
|--------|-----------------|---------------------|
| R² | 0.9293 | 0.9291 |
| Adjusted R² | 0.9234 | **0.9252** |

**Key insight:**

- **R²** always decreases when you drop a term — even if the term was useless
- **Adjusted R²** accounts for the number of predictors — it goes **up** when you drop an unhelpful term
- A small *increase* in Adjusted R² after dropping confirms the term wasn't contributing

> 💡 The Professor said: *"The adjusted R-squared is more appropriate here as it takes into account the number of terms in the model."*
> 
> ⚠️ **Exam tip:** Be able to explain why Adjusted R² is preferred when comparing models with different numbers of predictors.

## The Simpler (Final) Model

After dropping the interaction term (`Root:GrazingUngrazed`), we run `lm(Fruit ~ Root + Grazing, data = fruit)`:

```
Coefficients:
                 Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)      -127.829      9.664  -13.23  1.35e-15 ***
Root               23.560      1.149   20.51  < 2e-16 ***
GrazingUngrazed    36.103      3.357   10.75  6.11e-13 ***

Residual standard error: 6.747 on 37 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.9291,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9252
F-statistic: 242.3 on 2 and 37 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
```

All terms are now significant. The final model equations become:

**Grazed plants** (Z = 0):
$$\hat{\text{Fruit}} = -127.829 + 23.56 \cdot \text{Root}$$

**Ungrazed plants** (Z = 1):
$$\hat{\text{Fruit}} = -91.726 + 23.56 \cdot \text{Root}$$

*(Intercept for ungrazed = −127.829 + 36.103 = −91.726. The **slope is the same** for both groups!)*

> 💡 In the final model, both lines have the **same slope (23.56)** but different intercepts. Ungrazed plants have a higher intercept, meaning they produce more fruit at any given root size, when holding grazing constant.

---

# Professor's Exam Tips (From the Lecture Transcript)

The Professor explicitly mentioned what types of questions appear in exams. Here is a summary:

> 🎯 **From the transcript (direct exam guidance):**

1. **State the model** — Given a scenario or R output, write down the ANCOVA model equation using correct notation ($\beta_0 + \beta_1 X + ...$)

2. **Interpret coefficients** — For each coefficient: is it significant? Is it positive or negative? What does that mean in plain language (e.g., "for every one unit increase in Root, Fruit increases by 23.56 units, for grazed plants")?

3. **State the hypotheses** — Write out $H_0$ and $H_A$ for: the overall model, the interaction term, each main effect

4. **Know when to test the interaction first** — *"The first term you need to look at is the interaction term. Then, if it's not significant, you can interpret the main effects."*

5. **Remove the interaction term and rerun** — Know how to compare models using Adjusted R²

6. **Identify the experimental design** — Given an experiment description, identify: experimental units, factors, levels, treatments, covariate, response variable, and design type (CRD, single-factor, factorial, etc.)

7. **Interpret diagnostic plots** — Understand QQ plots, residuals vs fitted plots, and histograms of residuals

8. **Know R² vs Adjusted R²** — R² always decreases when you drop a term; Adjusted R² is what to look at when comparing models

> 💬 The Professor also said: *"If you look back at the past papers, you generally get the R output that you have looked at in R, and you have to put together the model or interpret the outputs."*
>
> **Action: Review the 2019 past exam paper (`STAT9004_Exam_19.pdf`) on Canvas.**

---

# Practical R Code Summary

Here is the key R code used in this analysis, which you'll replicate in the lab worksheet:

```r
# Load data
fruit <- read.table("fruit.txt", header = TRUE)

# Exploratory scatter plot
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(fruit, aes(x = Root, y = Fruit, colour = factor(Grazing))) +
  geom_point()

# Full ANCOVA model (with interaction)
model1 <- lm(Fruit ~ Root * Grazing, data = fruit)
summary(model1)

# Simpler model (without interaction)
model2 <- lm(Fruit ~ Root + Grazing, data = fruit)
summary(model2)

# Diagnostic plots
plot(model2)

# Check model assumptions
library(car)
leveneTest(Fruit ~ Grazing, data = fruit)  # homogeneity of variance
shapiro.test(residuals(model2))             # normality of residuals
```

> ⚠️ In the lab worksheet, you also work with the **Birds dataset** which requires **log transformations** (`log(AREA)`, `log(DIST)`). The Professor noted: *"With this exercise, you'll have to do some data transformations."*

---

# Summary Points

- **ANCOVA = ANOVA + a continuous covariate.** It handles models with both categorical and continuous predictors.
- The **categorical variable is the main interest**; the covariate is included to **control for** its effect on the response.
- The ANCOVA model is: $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 Z + \beta_3 XZ + e$, where X is continuous, Z is the dummy variable, and XZ is their interaction.
- **Always look at the interaction term first.** If it's not significant (p > 0.05), the slopes are parallel — consider removing it.
- The **Parsimony Principle** says simpler models are preferred when they fit equally well. Drop terms starting with the highest p-value.
- **R² always decreases when you drop a term** — use **Adjusted R²** to fairly compare models with different numbers of predictors.
- An *increase* in Adjusted R² after dropping a term is evidence that the term wasn't contributing.
- Assumptions are: **independence of errors, normality of errors, and equal variance.**
- In the exam: be ready to **state the model, interpret coefficients, write hypotheses, and compare models** from R output.

---

# Annotations Space

*Use this section to add your own notes, questions, and examples as you study.*

---

**My questions / things to clarify:**

\vspace{3cm}

---

**Additional examples I found helpful:**

\vspace{3cm}

---

**Things I need to review from earlier weeks (linked concepts):**

- One-Way ANOVA (Week 5)
- Two-Way ANOVA with interaction (Week 6/7)
- Simple Linear Regression (Week 8)
- Multiple Regression (Week 9)

\vspace{2cm}
